query: effectiveness of sotatercept in treating pulmonary arterial hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Initiating Therapy and Improving Patient Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Several new therapies are in clinical trials, some of which have the potential to be disease-modifying agents. These include sotatercept, a novel fusion protein that aims to re-balance the pro-proliferative and anti-proliferative pathways in the pulmonary vasculature. Also under investigation are targeted therapies for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH); and targeted therapies for right ventricular (RV) failure. [12] [13] [14] Prior research in combination therapies has created a paradigm shift in the treatment of PAH away from sequential therapy to combination therapy for newly diagnosed PAH patients. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: Several other new therapies are in clinical trials or are otherwise emerging. These include sotatercept, a novel fusion protein; targeted therapies for CTEPH; and targeted therapies for right ventricular (RV) failure. [12] [13] [14] Additionally, recent research into combination therapies has created a paradigm shift in the treatment of PAH. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) receptor type 2 (BMPR2) play a known role in the pathogenesis of PAH. [95] BMP is a member of the transforming growth factor (TGF)-beta family of proteins. [96] Sotatercept acts as a ligand trap for members of the TGF-beta superfamily and restores the balance between the growth-promoting activin pathway and the growth-inhibiting BMP pathway. [97]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: A total of 2 doses of sotatercept (0.3 or 0.7 mg/kg subcutaneously every three weeks) were compared with placebo in a 24-week multicenter trial of 106 patients with PAH receiving standard therapy. [98] Sotatercept reduced the PVR by 145.8 and 239 dyne/sec/cm for the 0.3 and 0.7 mg/kg dosing strategies, respectively. Exercise tolerance was also improved as measured by an increase in the 6MWD (29.4. [0.3 mg/kg] and 21.4 m [0.7 mg/kg]), and levels of BNP were also reduced. The most common AEs were headache, diarrhea, and peripheral edema. Of note, sotatercept resulted in thrombocytopenia and an increased hemoglobin level, and one patient in the sotatercept 0.7-mg group died from cardiac arrest. [98]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: Sotatercept binds activins and growth differentiation factors to restore the balance between growth-promoting and growth-inhibiting signaling pathways. In a 24-week multicenter randomized trial of 106 adults with PAH on background therapy, sotatercept treatment reduced pulmonary vascular resistance and was also associated with a decrease in N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide levels. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Several therapies are currently undergoing investigation in clinical trials for the treatment of PAH.

retrieved_docs.title: Scleroderma-Associated Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension: Early Detection for Better Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Macitentan is a dual ERA approved for the treatment of PAH and to delay disease progression. Approval was based on data from the Study with an Endothelin Receptor Antagonist in Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension to Improve Clinical Outcomes (SERAPHIN) trial, which compared macitentan 3 mg or 10 mg to placebo. [33] The primary outcome of SERAPHIN was a composite clinical outcome encompassing the risk of death and the risk of worsening of PAH. Patients enrolled in the trial were FC II to IV, with a 6MWD of >50 meters. Results indicated macitentan significantly reduced morbidity and mortality compared with placebo; both doses of macitentan also significantly reduced the risk for health-related quality of life (HRQoL) deterioration. At two years, macitentan was found to improve clinical outcomes by 45%. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Current Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: PAH represents an imbalance between vasoconstrictors and proliferative mediators (e.g., endothelin-1) and vasodilators (e.g., nitric oxide, prostacyclin). [42] Three key therapeutic approaches target either the nitric oxide, endothelin-1, or prostacyclin pathways (Table 7) . The emergence of targeted therapies for PAH has raised the 1-year survival rates to 91% and the 5-year survival rates to 61% for newly diagnosed patients. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: However, recent evidence now supports that patients with idiopathic PAH with no known autoimmune diseases also have auto-antibodies. Local immunoglobulin production may play a role in association with maladaptive immune responses in pulmonary tissue, and this provides a theoretical basis for future drug development and precision therapies in patients with PAH. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Current Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Macitentan— In the aforementioned SERAPHIN trial that compared macitentan with placebo in 250 patients with moderate-to-severe PAH (mostly WHO FC II and III), the benefits that were observed in the patients taking combined therapy (e.g., slower disease progression and lower mortality, improved exercise tolerance) were also seen in a subgroup of patients who were on single-agent therapy with macitentan. [57] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Current Treatment Options
retrieved_docs.content: Each of these medications has its own side effect profile and method of dosing and administration, and familiarity with the adverse events associated with each drug is necessary for timely recognition and optimal treatment of the patient with PAH.

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: Another pathway that has recently received attention as a potential therapeutic target in PAH is that of autoimmunity and inflammation. It has been widely recognized that patients with connective tissue diseases or viral infections—including COVID-19—are more susceptible to PAH. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Initiating Therapy and Improving Patient Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment options for PAH include prostacyclin therapies (intravenous epoprostenol; inhaled iloprost; intravenous, subcutaneous, inhaled, oral treprostinil, and selexipag), endothelin receptor antagonists (ambrisentan, bosentan, macitentan), phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, oral tadalafil), and one soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator (riociguat).

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Current Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with PAH who are typically non-vasoreactive (e.g., connective tissue disease-PAH) and have not undergone vasoreactive testing, and for patients who are non-vasoreactive or are vasoreactive and have failed CCB therapy, PAH-specific therapy is indicated. [3] The ACCP guidelines use the WHO FC to select suitable agents, while the 6th World Symposium on Pulmonary Hypertension utilizes the allocation of PAH-specific therapy based upon high risk and low or intermediate-risk categories. [45] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: The phase II Study of Angiogenic cell therapy for Progressive Pulmonary Hypertension: Intervention with Repeat dosing of eNOS-enhanced EPCs (SAPPHIRE) trial is evaluating the efficacy and safety of angiogenic therapy to restore the microvasculature with repeat dosing of autologous EPCs transfected with human eNOS in patients with refractory PAH. Forty-five patients will be randomized to receive four doses of eNOS gene-enhanced autologous EPCs monthly. The primary endpoint is the difference in 6MWD at six months between participants receiving the eNOS-EPCs compared to the placebo. This Canadian-based trial is currently recruiting patients; trial results are anticipated in 2023. (NCT03001414)

retrieved_docs.title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Current Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Macitentan plus sildenafil— The SERAPHIN trial compared the oral ERA, macitentan, with placebo in 250 patients with moderate-to-severe PAH; 85% of patients had IPAH or connective tissue disease-associated PAH, and the majority were FC II or III. Approximately 60% of patients were already on a PDE5I (i.e., combined therapy), mostly sildenafil, and 40% were treatment-naïve (i.e., single-agent therapy with macitentan). Over a 2-year period, fewer patients treated with macitentan (3 mg or 10 mg daily) progressed or died on therapy (38% and 31%, respectively, vs. 46%). Exercise capacity and WHO FC also improved with macitentan treatment. This benefit was observed independent of whether patients were on combination or single-agent oral therapy for PAH. [57] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Anagrelide is a pharmacologic agent whose mechanism of action involves inhibition of phosphodiesterase enzymes; it functions as a phosphodiesterase inhibitor, a property that underlies its therapeutic effects in relevant clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 486
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is categorized within the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC] and is described as an Immunomodulator (disposition), indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory agent. It is identified as a Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of targeting adhesion molecules. In addition, crizanlizumab is characterized as both imunomodulátory and imunosupresiva, underscoring its dual role as an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. This collection of classifications highlights crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune responses through selective adhesion molecule inhibition, which is pertinent to therapeutic strategies for inflammatory and vaso‑occlusive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen has a physiologic effect of decreasing protein synthesis, meaning that treatment with this antisense oligonucleotide leads to reduced synthesis of proteins in the body. This decrease in protein synthesis is a key aspect of mipomersen’s mechanism of action and contributes to its therapeutic and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox is classified as a member of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating its systemic use in the management of airway obstruction. Within the overarching therapeutic category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” which encompasses both systemic and inhaled agents, the subclass “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC” is a child of this broad class and is linked to the larger adrenergics‑for‑systemic‑use group, reflecting that many of its members act on adrenergic pathways. Systemic agents in this subclass include fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast and tezepelumab, representing mechanisms such as non‑selective β‑adrenergic activity (fenspiride), IL‑5‑targeting monoclonal antibodies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab), phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibition (roflumilast) and thymic stromal lymphopoietin blockade (tezepelumab) for asthma and related obstructive diseases. The inhaled branch is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, eprozinol, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, seratrodast and tezepelumab are members of the ATC subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” which falls under the broader class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES.” Within this systemic subgroup, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are biologic agents that inhibit interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin pathways; seratrodast is a thromboxane‑A₂ receptor antagonist; eprozinol and fenspiride possess antihistaminic and anti‑inflammatory properties; and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. The ATC hierarchy also contains an inhalant branch. The subclass “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants” includes anti‑inflammatory inhaled corticosteroids, which are organized as child entities: beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beclomethasone dipropionate (Czech: beklomethason dipropionát) and fluticasone propionate (Czech: flutikason propionát). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.
